questions.question:
The sum of the elements in each row of matrix M is 1. If b = Mx - x for some vector x (with b != 0), which one of the following statements is correct?

A. The equation Mx = b has no solution
B. The equation Mx = b has exactly two solutions
C. The equation Mx = b has infinitely many solutions
D. The equation Mx = b has a unique solution
questions.answer: C. The equation Mx = b has infinitely many solutions

understand.plain: A row-stochastic matrix M has eigenvalue 1 (since Me = e where e is the all-ones vector), so det(M - I) = 0 and M - I is singular. Writing b = Mx - x = (M - I)x shows b is already in the column space of M - I, making the system Mx = b consistent. A consistent linear system with a singular coefficient matrix always has infinitely many solutions, because any vector in the null space of M - I can be added to a particular solution.

keywords.explain: A square matrix where every row sums to 1; this forces 1 to be an eigenvalue with eigenvector (1,1,...,1)^T.
When 1 is an eigenvalue of M, the matrix M - I has determinant 0 and a nontrivial null space.
keywords.example: Every row of M - I sums to 0, so the rows are linearly dependent.

keywords.explain: If the coefficient matrix is singular but b is in its column space, the solution is x_particular + null(M - I), an infinite affine set.
keywords.example: Adding any scalar multiple of the all-ones vector to a solution gives another solution.

understand.visual_alt: A row-stochastic matrix M showing that each row sums to 1, implying M - I is singular.

given.aim: Determine the nature of solutions to Mx = b when b = (M - I)x and M is row-stochastic.

terms.meaning: Square matrix where every row sums to 1.
terms.example: Me = e where e = [1,1,...,1]^T, so e is an eigenvector of M with eigenvalue 1.

terms.meaning: det(M - I) = 0; the null space of M - I contains at least the vector e.
terms.example: (M - I)e = Me - e = e - e = 0, confirming e is in null(M - I).
terms.connects: Singular coefficient matrix means solutions, when they exist, come in infinite families.

terms.term: b in column space of M - I
terms.meaning: Since b = (M - I)x, b is the image of x under M - I, so it lies in range(M - I).
terms.example: x itself satisfies (M - I)x = b, proving the system is consistent.

given.plan: Step 1: Show M being row-stochastic implies eigenvalue 1, so M - I is singular. Step 2: Show b = (M - I)x is in the range of M - I, making the system consistent. Step 3: Conclude infinitely many solutions from consistent + singular.

steps.title: Eigenvalue 1 from row-stochastic property
steps.apply: Each row of M sums to 1, so M * e = e. This means e is an eigenvector of M with eigenvalue 1. Therefore 1 is an eigenvalue of M, which implies det(M - I) = 0, making M - I a singular matrix with a nontrivial null space.
steps.note: This eigenvalue argument holds for any square row-stochastic matrix of any size.

steps.title: b lies in range(M - I), so the system is consistent
steps.formula_raw: b = (M - I)x  =>  b in range(M - I)
steps.apply: Given b = Mx - x = (M - I)x, b is the image of the specific vector x under M - I. Thus x is already a particular solution to (M - I)y = b, confirming the system Mx = b is consistent (has at least one solution).
steps.note: b being in range(M - I) is guaranteed by definition, regardless of which x was used.

steps.title: Consistent + singular => infinitely many solutions
steps.apply: Since M - I is singular, its null space is nontrivial (contains at least e, the all-ones vector). For any scalar t, the vector x + t*e is also a solution to (M - I)y = b: (M - I)(x + te) = (M - I)x + t*(M - I)e = b + t*0 = b. There are infinitely many such solutions, one for each value of t.
steps.note: Answer is C. Options A and D are impossible since b is consistent; option B (exactly two) is impossible for a linear system.

solution.result: C. The equation Mx = b has infinitely many solutions.

questions.formula_note: The row-stochastic property forces eigenvalue 1 on M, making M - I singular and guaranteeing infinitely many solutions whenever the system is consistent.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements is/are TRUE? (R denotes the set of real numbers.)

A. There exist M in R^(3x3), p in R^3, and q in R^3 such that Mx = p has a unique solution and Mx = q has no solution.
B. There exist M in R^(3x3), p in R^3, and q in R^3 such that Mx = p has no solution and Mx = q has infinite solutions.
C. There exist M in R^(3x3), p in R^3, and q in R^3 such that Mx = p has a unique solution and Mx = q has infinite solutions.
D. There exist M in R^(3x3), p in R^3, and q in R^3 such that Mx = p has no solution and Mx = q has infinite solutions.

understand.plain: For a fixed matrix M, the type of solution (unique, no solution, infinite) depends on the rank of M and whether the right-hand side vector is in the column space. If M is invertible (full rank 3), every system Mx = b has exactly one solution, so you cannot get no-solution or infinite solutions for any right-hand side. If M is singular (rank less than 3), some right-hand sides lie outside the column space (giving no solution) and others lie inside (giving infinitely many solutions). Statements B and D both correctly describe this achievable scenario; statements A and C require unique solution for one right-hand side, which forces full rank, contradicting the other part.

keywords.explain: The dimension of the column space; rank 3 means invertible and every system has a unique solution.
keywords.example: A 3x3 identity matrix has rank 3; every system Ix = b has unique solution x = b.

keywords.explain: The set of all vectors that can be written as Mx for some x; a system Mx = b is consistent iff b is in the column space.
keywords.example: If M has rank 2, its column space is a 2D plane; a vector off that plane makes an inconsistent system.

keywords.term: no solution + infinite solutions with same M
keywords.explain: Possible only for singular M: take p outside and q inside the column space.
keywords.example: M = diag(1,1,0): p = (0,0,1)^T gives no solution; q = (1,0,0)^T gives infinitely many solutions.

understand.visual_alt: Column space of singular M as a plane; p lies off the plane (no solution) and q lies on the plane (infinitely many solutions).

given.aim: Determine which combinations of solution types for two different right-hand sides p and q (same matrix M) are achievable.

terms.meaning: Exactly one x solves the system, which requires M to be invertible (rank n = 3 for 3x3).
terms.example: If M is invertible, x = M^(-1)p is the unique solution.
terms.connects: An invertible M forces unique solution for every b, ruling out no-solution or infinite solutions.

terms.meaning: p is not in the column space of M; requires M to be singular.
terms.example: M = [[1,0,0],[0,1,0],[0,0,0]], p = [0,0,1]^T: third equation 0 = 1 is impossible.
terms.connects: Singular M can simultaneously have some right-hand sides inside (consistent) and outside (inconsistent) the column space.

terms.meaning: q is in the column space of M but M is singular, so null(M) is nontrivial.
terms.example: M as above, q = [1,0,0]^T: solutions are x = [1,0,t]^T for any t in R.
terms.connects: When M is singular and q is in col(M), the solution set is a coset of null(M).

given.plan: Show that unique solution requires full rank, which contradicts infinite or no solution for any other right-hand side (eliminating A and C). Then construct a concrete singular M to demonstrate that no solution and infinite solutions can coexist for different right-hand sides (confirming B and D).

steps.title: Unique solution requires M to be invertible
steps.formula_raw: rank(M) = 3 <=> Mx = b has unique solution for every b in R^3
steps.apply: If Mx = p has a unique solution, then the system has no free variables and M must have rank 3 (full rank). A full-rank 3x3 matrix is invertible. For an invertible M, Mx = b has a unique solution x = M^(-1)b for every b in R^3. Therefore Mx = q also has a unique solution for any q, making it impossible to have infinite solutions or no solution for q with the same M. Statements A and C are FALSE.
steps.note: The key insight: solution type is a property of M (not just b) when M is invertible.

steps.title: Singular M allows both no solution and infinite solutions
steps.formula_raw: M singular: some b in col(M) => infinite solutions; some b not in col(M) => no solution
steps.apply: Let M = [[1,0,0],[0,1,0],[0,0,0]]. The column space is the xy-plane (all vectors with third component 0). Choose p = [0,0,1]^T: Mx = p requires 0 = 1, so no solution exists. Choose q = [1,1,0]^T (in the column space): Mx = q has solutions [1,1,t]^T for any t in R, giving infinitely many solutions. This example proves statements B and D are TRUE.
steps.note: Both B and D are correct; they describe the same achievable scenario (no solution for p, infinite for q) for a singular M.

solution.result: B and D are TRUE. Statements A and C are FALSE because a unique solution for any right-hand side forces M to be invertible, which then requires unique solutions for all right-hand sides.

questions.formula_note: The Rouche-Capelli theorem states that Ax = b is consistent iff rank(A) = rank([A|b]); a consistent system has a unique solution iff rank equals the number of unknowns.

questions.question:
What values of x, y and z satisfy the following system of linear equations?

[1  2  3] [x]   [6 ]
[2  1  2] [y] = [10]
[1  1  1] [z]   [8 ]

A. x = 6,  y = 3,  z = 2
B. x = 12, y = 3,  z = -4
C. x = 6,  y = 6,  z = -4
D. x = 12, y = -3, z = 0

understand.plain: The three equations are x + 2y + 3z = 6, 2x + y + 2z = 10, and x + y + z = 8. We apply Gaussian elimination (row operations) to reduce the augmented matrix to upper triangular form, then use back substitution to find z, then y, then x. The system has a unique solution.

keywords.explain: The coefficient matrix M combined with the right-hand side vector b into a single matrix [M|b] for row operations.
keywords.example: For x + 2y = 5, 3x + y = 7: augmented matrix is [[1,2,5],[3,1,7]].

keywords.explain: Systematic row operations (multiply a row by a scalar, add a multiple of one row to another) to achieve upper triangular form.
keywords.example: R2 = R2 - 2*R1 eliminates x from equation 2.

keywords.explain: Starting from the last equation of the triangular system, solve for each variable in reverse order.
keywords.example: If 2z = -8 then z = -4; substitute into prior equation to find y, then x.

understand.visual_alt: Augmented matrix after Gaussian elimination showing upper triangular form before back substitution.

given.aim: Solve the 3x3 linear system: x + 2y + 3z = 6, 2x + y + 2z = 10, x + y + z = 8.

terms.connects: Used as the pivot row to eliminate x from equations 2 and 3.
After R3 = R3 - R1, becomes 0x - y - 2z = 2; used to eliminate y from equation 2.

given.plan: Write the augmented matrix. Eliminate x by subtracting 2*R1 from R2 and R1 from R3. Then eliminate y by subtracting 3*R3 from R2. Solve for z by back substitution, then y, then x.

steps.title: Form augmented matrix and eliminate x
steps.note: x is now eliminated from equations 2 and 3.

steps.title: Eliminate y from equation 2
steps.note: Swapped rows 2 and 3 order for clarity; the triangular form makes back substitution straightforward.

steps.title: Back substitution to find z, y, x
steps.apply: From row 3: 2z = -8, so z = -4. From row 2 (R3 after swap): -y - 2(-4) = 2 => -y + 8 = 2 => y = 6. From row 1: x + 2(6) + 3(-4) = 6 => x + 12 - 12 = 6 => x = 6. Solution: x = 6, y = 6, z = -4.
steps.note: Verify: eq1: 6+12-12=6 Y; eq2: 12+6-8=10 Y; eq3: 6+6-4=8 Y. Answer is C.

questions.formula_note: Gaussian elimination with back substitution solves a nonsingular 3x3 system in O(n^3) time; the pivot operations preserve the solution set.

questions.question:
A unit vector perpendicular to both the vectors a = 2i - j - k and b = -i + 2j - k is:

A. (1/sqrt(3))(i + j + k)
B. (1/sqrt(3))(i - j - k)
C. (1/sqrt(3))(i + j - k)
D. (1/sqrt(3))(i - j + k)

understand.plain: A vector perpendicular to two given vectors is their cross product. We compute a x b using the determinant formula with i, j, k in the first row. The result is (3, 3, 3) which is proportional to (1, 1, 1). Dividing by its magnitude 3*sqrt(3) gives the unit vector (1/sqrt(3))(i + j + k). We can verify by checking that this unit vector has dot product zero with both a and b.

keywords.explain: a x b gives a vector perpendicular to both a and b; computed via the 3x3 determinant with i, j, k in the top row.
A vector with magnitude exactly 1, obtained by dividing any nonzero vector by its magnitude.
Two vectors are perpendicular iff their dot product equals zero.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='320' height='210' viewBox='0 0 320 210' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='320' height='210' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='160' y='26' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>a x b = Perpendicular Vector</text><defs><marker id='va' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='#7F77DD'/></marker><marker id='vb' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='#1D9E75'/></marker><marker id='vc' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='#D85A30'/></marker></defs><line x1='160' y1='130' x2='90' y2='80' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5' marker-end='url(#va)'/><line x1='160' y1='130' x2='230' y2='80' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2.5' marker-end='url(#vb)'/><line x1='160' y1='130' x2='160' y2='45' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2.5' marker-end='url(#vc)'/><text x='78' y='75' class='sub' fill='#7F77DD'>a</text><text x='232' y='75' class='sub' fill='#1D9E75'>b</text><text x='165' y='42' class='sub' fill='#D85A30'>a x b</text><text x='160' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>a = 2i - j - k</text><text x='160' y='172' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>b = -i + 2j - k</text><text x='160' y='189' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>a x b = 3i + 3j + 3k</text><text x='160' y='206' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>unit = (1/sqrt(3))(i+j+k)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three vectors from a common origin: a and b in a plane, and their cross product a x b pointing perpendicular upward.

given.aim: Find the unit vector perpendicular to both a = 2i - j - k and b = -i + 2j - k.

terms.example: a . (1,1,1) = 2-1-1 = 0, so (1,1,1) is perpendicular to a.
terms.connects: One of the two vectors that define the plane; a x b gives the normal to this plane.

terms.example: b . (1,1,1) = -1+2-1 = 0, so (1,1,1) is also perpendicular to b.
terms.connects: The second vector in the plane; along with a defines the direction of the cross product.

terms.meaning: a x b = det([[i,j,k],[a1,a2,a3],[b1,b2,b3]]) expands by cofactors along the first row.
terms.connects: Computing all three components gives (3, 3, 3), proportional to (1,1,1).

given.plan: Compute a x b using the determinant formula. Find the magnitude of the result. Divide by the magnitude to get the unit vector. Verify with dot products.

steps.title: Compute a x b component by component
steps.note: All three components are equal, confirming the cross product points in the (1,1,1) direction.

steps.title: Compute magnitude and normalise
steps.apply: |a x b| = sqrt(9 + 9 + 9) = sqrt(27) = 3*sqrt(3). Unit vector = (3i + 3j + 3k) / (3*sqrt(3)) = (1/sqrt(3))(i + j + k).
steps.note: This is option A. The factor 1/sqrt(3) ~ 0.577 ensures the magnitude equals 1.

steps.title: Verify perpendicularity via dot products
steps.apply: u = (1/sqrt(3))(1,1,1). u . a = (1/sqrt(3))(1*2 + 1*(-1) + 1*(-1)) = (1/sqrt(3))(2-1-1) = 0. u . b = (1/sqrt(3))(1*(-1) + 1*2 + 1*(-1)) = (1/sqrt(3))(-1+2-1) = 0. Both dot products are zero, confirming u is perpendicular to both a and b.

questions.formula_note: The cross product a x b gives a vector perpendicular to both a and b; dividing by its magnitude gives the unit normal vector to the plane spanned by a and b.
